Agent behavior is determined more by prompts than by code — a small prompt change can dramatically alter output quality and decision patterns. Tracking prompt hashes in the version registry means you can correlate any behavioral change with exactly which deployment introduced it.

PRODUCTION READINESS CHECKLIST 

Before You Go Live: Production Readiness Checklist

 

Checklist Item

Verification

Health monitoring

AGENT_RUN_METRICS table deployed, baseline metrics captured from staging

Cost controls

Token budget limits set, soft and hard limits configured, cost alerts enabled

Failure handling

Retry logic on all LLM calls, fallback model configured, safe halt on state corruption

LangSmith tracing

LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2 enabled, project name set, traces verified in LangSmith UI

HANA Cloud audit trail

AI_AUDIT.AGENT_DECISIONS table deployed, all human-in-the-loop nodes logging

SAP Cloud Logging

Structured JSON logs configured, Cloud Logging service bound to BTP CF app

Multi-landscape config

All sensitive values in environment variables, no hardcoded credentials

Separate resource groups

DEV, QA, PROD resource groups created in AI Core, isolated from each other

Version registry

AI_OPS.AGENT_VERSIONS table deployed, initial version registered at deployment

Rollback plan

Previous version deployable within 15 minutes, rollback procedure documented

Escalation contacts

ESCALATION_MATRIX configured with real email addresses per exception type

Approval SLAs defined

Timeout values set per approval type, fallback strategies configured

Series Complete — What You've Built Across Six Parts

Across six articles, this series built a complete foundation for agentic AI in SAP:

  • Part 1: Agentic AI concepts and your first LangGraph agent on SAP AI Core
  • Part 2: ReAct agents with SAP tools — dynamic reasoning and tool selection
  • Part 3: Multi-agent orchestration — coordinating specialized SAP agents
  • Part 4: Five migration agents — assessment, validation, runbooks, monitoring, reconciliation
  • Part 5: Human-in-the-loop patterns — approval, escalation, audit trail
  • Part 6: Production operations — health, cost, failure, observability, scaling, versioning

The architecture that connects all six parts is not theoretical. Every pattern here has been applied to real SAP workstreams — ECC to S/4HANA migration, HANA Cloud transformation, DMLT workstreams, change request management. The code is production-ready with appropriate caveats for your specific landscape configuration.

If you've followed this series and built something — share it in the comments. The community variations are where the most valuable refinements emerge. And if you're just starting, Part 1 is the entry point. The full series builds progressively — each part assumes the previous one.

entry.title: Integrate CAP application as outbound channel for SAP Document AI
entry.content:
The Challenge

When integrating SAP Document AI as a document extraction engine into a broader BTP solution, one of the first hurdles you’ll encounter is the outbound callback mechanism. Document AI supports notifying an external system once extraction is complete — a great feature for building event-driven, asynchronous document processing pipelines.

However, there’s a subtle but important quirk: Document AI unconditionally performs a CSRF token preflight before sending the actual extraction result callback. It fires a GET request with the header X-CSRF-Token: Fetch to your callback endpoint regardless of whether CSRF protection is actually in place on the receiving end.

This behavior cannot be disabled in the Document AI configuration.

The solution? Register Express middleware directly during CAP’s bootstrap phase — before the framework takes over — and handle both the preflight and the callback there.

Architecture Overview

SAP Document AI
      │
      │  ① GET /callback/notify
      │     X-CSRF-Token: Fetch
      │◄─── X-CSRF-Token: <random-token>
      │
      │  ② POST /callback/notify
      │     X-CSRF-Token: <random-token>
      │     Body: notification payload
      ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
│          CAP Application             │
│                                      │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │     csrf-middleware.js         │  │
│  │  • Issues token on GET/HEAD    │  │
│  │  • Validates token on POST     │  │
│  └───────────────┬────────────────┘  │
│                  │                   │
│  ┌───────────────▼────────────────┐  │
│  │     callback-service.js        │  │
│  │  • Parses extraction payload   │  │
│  │  • Matches & finalises job     │  │
│  └────────────────────────────────┘  │
└──────────────────────────────────────┘

The middleware intercepts both requests before CAP’s own routing and CSRF logic runs, acting as a transparent proxy layer between Document AI and your business logic.

Implementation Walkthrough

1. Hooking into the CAP Bootstrap

The key to this entire approach is cds.on('bootstrap', (app) => { ... }). This event fires after CAP has initialized the underlying Express application but before it registers its own middleware stack. This gives us direct, unmediated access to the raw Express app object.

The server.js is deliberately thin. Its only responsibility is wiring the CSRF middleware into the Express stack at the right moment:

'use strict';

const cds = require('@sap/cds');
const csrfMiddleware = require('./csrf-middleware');

cds.on('bootstrap', (app) => {
    app.use(csrfMiddleware);
});

module.exports = cds.server;

⚠️Exporting cds.server is essential — this tells CAP to use the standard server startup while still respecting your bootstrap customizations.

The actual callback business logic lives separately in callback-service.js, keeping concerns cleanly separated. The middleware handles the HTTP-level CSRF handshake; the service handles what to do with the payload.

2. The CSRF Middleware

The full middleware lives in csrf-middleware.js and handles two responsibilities: issuing tokens on preflight requests and validating tokens on incoming POST requests.

Token Lifecycle

Rather than returning a static or meaningless string, the middleware generates a cryptographically random token per preflight, stores it in memory with a TTL, and enforces single-use consumption:

// In-memory token store: token → expiry timestamp
const _tokens = new Map();
const TOKEN_TTL_MS = 10 * 60 * 1000; // 10 minutes

function generateToken() {
    const token = crypto.randomBytes(24).toString('hex');
    _tokens.set(token, Date.now() + TOKEN_TTL_MS);
    return token;
}

function validateToken(token) {
    if (!token) return false;
    const expiry = _tokens.get(token);
    if (!expiry) return false;
    if (Date.now() > expiry) { _tokens.delete(token); return false; }
    _tokens.delete(token); // single-use: consumed on first valid POST
    return true;
}

A periodic cleanup interval ensures expired tokens don’t accumulate indefinitely:

setInterval(() => {
    const now = Date.now();
    for (const [token, expiry] of _tokens.entries()) {
        if (expiry < now) _tokens.delete(token);
    }
}, 60000);

The Middleware Function

The middleware is path-scoped to /callback/* routes and handles each HTTP method appropriately:

function csrfMiddleware(req, res, next) {
    // Only apply to /callback paths
    if (!req.path.startsWith('/callback')) return next();

    const csrfHeader = req.headers['x-csrf-token'];

    // GET or HEAD preflight: issue a fresh token
    if ((req.method === 'GET' || req.method === 'HEAD') 
            && csrfHeader?.toLowerCase() === 'fetch') {
        const token = generateToken();
        log.debug(`CSRF preflight (${req.method}): issued token for ${req.path}`);
        res.set('X-CSRF-Token', token);
        res.set('Allow', 'GET,HEAD,POST');
        res.set('Cache-Control', 'no-cache, no-store');
        return res.status(200).end();
    }

    // POST: validate the token if present
    if (req.method === 'POST') {
        if (csrfHeader && !validateToken(csrfHeader)) {
            log.warn(`CSRF validation failed for POST ${req.path}`);
            return res.status(403).json({ 
                error: { message: 'CSRF token validation failed' } 
            });
        }
        log.debug(`CSRF token accepted for POST ${req.path}`);
    }

    next();
}

3. Handling the Callback Payload

Once the CSRF handshake is complete, Document AI posts the extraction results to /callback/notify. The actual processing logic is registered as a standard CAP service endpoint in callback-service.js.

What Document AI Sends

This is where it gets interesting. SAP Document AI schemas have a toggle that controls how much data is included directly in the callback payload. Depending on your schema configuration, you’ll encounter two patterns:

Option A — Full extraction result in the callback body

When the "include extraction result" toggle is enabled on your Document AI schema, the callback payload contains the complete extraction result directly in the DocumentResult field — serialized as a Python dictionary string representation (not valid JSON). This means you get all extracted field values, confidence scores, and metadata in a single push, with no need to make a follow-up API call to retrieve results.

DocumentResult = "{file_ID=abc-123, extracted_fields=[...], schema=InvoiceSchema, ...}"

⚠️ Note that this is Python repr() format, not JSON. Key-value pairs use = instead of :, and null values appear as None. Your callback handler needs to account for this — either with a dedicated parser or targeted regex extraction for the fields you need.

Option B — Lightweight notification only

When the toggle is disabled (or a simpler integration is preferred), the callback only contains a minimal notification with the NotificationObjectId. You can then use this ID to fetch the structured extraction result on demand via the Document AI DocumentService:

GET /document-service/v1/DocumentService/Documents({NotificationObjectId})/DocumentService.simpleStructure()

The DocumentService.simpleStructure() endpoint returns a clean, normalized JSON representation of the extracted fields — often the more practical choice if you don’t need the full raw extraction metadata and want a consistent, version-stable data contract between Document AI and your CAP application.

Setting Up the Callback Destination

With the CSRF middleware in place, the next step is making the callback URL reachable from SAP Document AI. This involves two things: defining the destination in your mta.yaml so it is automatically provisioned on deployment (ensuring automatic client credential updates), and then registering it as an outbound channel in the Document AI schema configuration UI.

Defining the Destination in mta.yaml

SAP BTP Destination Service allows you to declare destinations declaratively as part of your MTA deployment descriptor. This means the callback destination is created or updated automatically every time you deploy — no manual destination configuration in the BTP cockpit required.

Add a dedicated content deployer module to your mta.yaml:

# ─── Destination content deployer ─────────────────────────────────────────────
- name: docai-cap-dest-content
  type: com.sap.application.content
  requires:
    - name: docai-cap-dest # the name of your destination resource
      parameters:
        content-target: true
    - name: docai-cap-xsuaa # the name of your xsuaa resource
      parameters:
        service-key:
          name: docai-cap-xsuaa-key
    - name: srv-api
  parameters:
    content:
      subaccount:
        existing_destinations_policy: update
        destinations:
          - Name: CAP_DocAI_Callback
            Description: Callback destination for CAP app
            URL: ~{srv-url}/callback/notify
            Authentication: OAuth2ClientCredentials
            TokenServiceInstanceName: docai-cap-xsuaa
            TokenServiceKeyName: docai-cap-xsuaa-key
            HTML5.DynamicDestination: true
  build-parameters:
    no-source: tru

A few things worth calling out:

~{srv-api/srv-url}/callback/notify
The URL is not hardcoded — it is resolved dynamically at deployment time using the srv-api provided parameters of your CAP server module. This means the destination always points to the correct URL regardless of which BTP space or region you deploy to, making the descriptor fully portable across landscapes.

OAuth2ClientCredentials
The callback destination is secured with OAuth2 Client Credentials, backed by the XSUAA instance of your CAP application. When Document AI calls the callback URL, it first fetches a Bearer token from XSUAA using the client credentials stored in docai-cap-scanner-xsuaa-key, and includes it in the Authorization header of every request — both the CSRF preflight GET and the actual callback POST. Your CAP application’s standard XSUAA-based authentication middleware validates this token automatically.

existing_destinations_policy: update
This ensures that redeployments update the destination in place rather than failing on a name conflict. Safe to use in CI/CD pipelines.

HTML5.DynamicDestination: true
Marks the destination as dynamically resolvable, which is required for runtime URL resolution via the destination service.

Configuring the Outbound Channel in SAP Document AI

Once deployed, the destination is available in your BTP subaccount and can be referenced directly from the SAP Document AI configuration UI. Navigate to Channels in Document AI and create an Outbound Channel ensuring the toggle for "Use SAP BTP Destination" is enabled.

 

Once saved, go to your schema, navigate to the Outbound Channel Configuration and add the just configured channel and toggle on/off the inclusion of your extraction results. Specifying the Document Status in which the channel shall be triggered allows you to either immediately forward the notification once the extraction is done (statusReview Needed) or only after a human validation (e.g. status Confirmed) .

With both the destination provisioned automatically via MTA and the outbound channel registered in the Document AI schema UI, the entire integration is reproducible from a single mbt build && cf deploy — no manual post-deployment steps required.

Conclusion

SAP Document AI is a powerful extraction service, but its fixed CSRF preflight behavior creates a real integration challenge when connecting to CAP applications or Integration Flows. By hooking into CAP’s bootstrap lifecycle and registering a focused, well-structured Express middleware, we can elegantly satisfy Document AI’s handshake requirements without patching CAP internals or weakening the application’s overall security posture.

The result is a clean two-layer architecture: the middleware owns the CSRF token lifecycle at the HTTP layer, and the callback service owns the business logic of matching jobs and processing extraction results — each with a single, clear responsibility.

This pattern is also broadly reusable: any time you need to expose a raw HTTP endpoint in a CAP application that bypasses the standard framework middleware (for webhooks, health checks, or third-party callbacks with unusual requirements), cds.on('bootstrap') is your entry point.

Happy building! 🚀

entry.title: Building AI Tools and Agents for SAP CX Solutions
entry.content:
We’re pleased to announce the availability of a new live session on SAP Learning that shows SAP CX Solutions by building AI tools and agents.

This live session provides an introduction into how to design, build, and deploy AI-powered tools and intelligent agents for SAP Customer Experience (CX) solutions using the SAP CX AI Toolkit. Participants will see practical examples of virtual assistants, recommendation engines, and automated agents, and learn how the toolkit accelerates development, integration, and governance across Sales, Service, and Commerce scenarios.

 

Learning Outcome

After participating in this live session, you will understand:

 

  • Core capabilities of the SAP CX AI Toolkit and how it fits into the SAP CX landscape
  • Typical AI agent types for CX (conversational assistants, recommendation engines, intent classifiers, workflow automations)
  • End-to-end development pattern: data sources → model selection → training/tuning → deployment → monitoring
  • Integration points with SAP CX modules and common extension approaches
  • Key considerations for data privacy, security, explainability, and compliance in CX AI
  • Best practices for governance, testing, and measuring business impact

 

Why Attend a Live Session?

If your current experience in this topic is intermediate and you are willing to have an interactive learning experience, this course is a great fit for you.

 

Register Today

Register today to start building the skills that will move you forward.

 

👉You can find the Registration link here:

https://learning.sap.com/live-sessions/building-ai-tools-and-agents-for-sap-cx-solutions?searchId=8d5279b3-a701-4eeb-9ca3-07004e2c5d26&listPosition=7

entry.title: Why SAP Document AI Matters More Than Most Enterprises Think
entry.content:
Introduction

When everyone gets excited about the same topics, conversations will naturally center around them. Eventually, that excitement will catch on, and you’ll find yourself on the same wavelength as everyone else. Currently, as businesses focus on enterprise AI projects, most discussions revolve around similar themes: copilot, agents, and large language models. However, despite significant investments in AI, many businesses still face a common operational obstacle: documents.

Regardless of the industry, invoices, purchase orders, supplier forms, shipping documents, contracts, and compliance documents flow between departments daily. Each requires some degree of manual verification or validation and often involves multiple systems or workflows. This is where SAP Document AI comes in.

SAP Document AI transforms unstructured business documents into structured operational data, helping businesses modernize their operations. Instead of just digitizing your documents, you can use Document AI to make them useful and functional within your business. Even with advancements in intelligent front-end experiences, the foundational infrastructure supporting these solutions still relies on manual verification, various data extraction methods, and disconnected workflows. Document intelligence within SAP environments has matured, and companies now view it as a key automation initiative rather than a secondary option.

Our Experience

Adopting Document AI can reduce the need for labor to support business functions, leading to more efficient solutions. SAP Document AI addresses a common challenge in enterprise environments: extracting, understanding, and organizing information from business documents. In many companies, important operational data arrives in formats like PDFs, scanned files, emails, invoices, and forms, which ERP systems struggle to process without manual help.

SAP Document AI lowers this friction by using AI to identify document types, extract relevant fields, classify information, and prepare that data for business workflows. This means organizations can automate many document-heavy processes across finance, procurement, supply chain, HR, and other operational areas, rather than having employees manually review documents and transfer information between systems. The value of using Document AI goes beyond faster document processing; it empowers operational workflows to operate with greater speed, consistency, and scalability. As enterprises manage more operational data, minimizing manual document reliance becomes essential for maintaining efficiency.

We had the opportunity to see this first-hand. In this scenario, the company was operating in the oil and gas industry, where customer representatives and field engineers constantly generate operational documents while working across remote locations. Every service ticket, carrier document, export filing, client approval, invoice, or credit memo needed to be attached to the correct Sales Order inside SAP for compliance and operational traceability purposes. The challenge was that these documents arrived through emails in different languages, layouts, and formats, often with little context beyond a subject line. As a result, employees had to manually open every file, identify what type of document it was, locate the Sales Order reference somewhere inside the content, rename the file according to internal naming conventions, and upload it into SAP. At a scale of more than 8,000 documents per month, this translated into over 1,000 hours of manual effort every month, distributed across teams in multiple regions.

The organization already had partial automation in place through legacy capture tools and batch jobs, but the process still depended heavily on human intervention before the automation could even begin. A single typo in a file name or an incorrectly classified document could silently break the workflow and prevent the document from being linked correctly inside SAP. By implementing SAP Document AI on SAP BTP, the process shifted from manual interpretation to intelligent document processing. Incoming documents were automatically picked up through SAP Integration Suite, classified using SAP Document AI, and processed to extract key operational data such as Sales Order numbers, vendor details, amounts, and document dates. The extracted information was then validated and routed directly into SAP workflows, significantly reducing manual effort while improving scalability, consistency, and operational visibility across the entire process.

Why This Matters Beyond Document Processing

What made this project valuable was not just the automation itself. I would say it was rather the impact it had on daily operations. Once documents could be processed and validated in a more consistent way, teams spent far less time trying to interpret files manually or fixing small mistakes that slowed everything down. Workflows became easier to manage across different regions, and the process no longer depended so heavily on people knowing specific naming conventions or internal steps that were never formally documented.

Over time, we also realized that SAP Document AI was doing much more than helping with document processing. It became part of the operational structure supporting the entire workflow. As companies continue investing in SAP Business AI and intelligent workflows, having reliable operational data becomes increasingly important. If documents are still disconnected from the systems around them, scaling automation becomes much harder than expected.

Conclusion

As we said at the beginning, a lot of the conversation around enterprise AI focuses on what people can immediately see: copilots, AI agents, chat interfaces, and new user experiences. At the same time, many operational problems still happen behind the scenes, especially in processes where important business information is buried inside emails, PDFs, scanned files, and manual workflows.

Our experience with SAP Document AI showed us that document intelligence is no longer just a secondary automation tool. It can play an important role in helping businesses create cleaner and more scalable operations. When documents become structured operational data instead of isolated files, workflows move faster, teams spend less time on repetitive tasks, and enterprise systems become easier to scale over time.

entry.title: Join the SAP SuccessFactors AI User Group
entry.content:
AI is transforming HR faster than ever and staying connected to the conversation matters.

The new SAP SuccessFactors AI User Group is a global, customer-only space designed to help HR professionals stay engaged in AI discussions year-round through peer collaboration, shared insights, and direct access to SAP experts.

Inside the group, customers can:

  • Ask questions and exchange best practices with peers in a GDPR-protected environment (S-user/Universal ID required)
  • Engage directly with SAP HCM AI experts and consultants
  • Discover AI use cases, tips, and ongoing discussions without waiting for the next webinar or event
  • Help build a growing knowledge base the broader customer community can learn from

Whether you're just beginning your AI journey or looking to expand adoption across your organization, this group provides an always-on space to learn, collaborate, and stay informed.

We look forward to seeing you in the conversation.

Please note: Membership is available exclusively to SAP customers. Partners are welcome to join only if they are also an SAP customer with a valid S-user ID. If you experience any issues accessing the group or validating your S-user ID, please contact the SAP Customer Interaction Center (CIC) for assistance.

entry.title: What We Learned Hosting a SAP Document AI Workshop
entry.content:
Introduction

Some weeks ago we had a hands-on event focused on SAP Document AI and enterprise document automation. We had planned it to be just a technical workshop, yet the attendees wasted no time in telling us all kinds of complications they were experiencing at the time. Soon, we were talking about operational processes, workflow complexity, and the increasing importance of AI in enterprise environments.

When we were planning the workshop we did not just want to show people a product. Our goal was to create a space where participants could engage directly with actual document processing situations, as well as investigate automation options that might enhance their routine workflows. I mean, you can't have an opinion about something you haven't used or experienced yourself.

During the session attendees had the opportunity to experiment with document classification, extraction models, workflow routing and automation processes related to larger SAP workflows.

As the Hackathon went on, the conversations naturally shifted toward some of the operational challenges many companies are currently facing. Although SAP Document AI was the focus, there was also room for repetitive manual work, fragmented validation processes, inconsistent document formats, and the difficulty of scaling document-heavy operations across teams and business units.

 

What the workshop was about

The workshop included product exploration and practical activities, simulating real enterprise scenarios. Rather than merely attend presentations or demos, participants were able to directly engage with workflows, classifiers, extraction schemas, and automation processes.

The event provided participants with insights into how SAP Document AI can support invoicing processes, free-form documents, workflow routing, and document classification.

 

One exercise that participants seemed to find particularly useful was the ROI calculator we brought to the workshop. While most of the day focused on workflows and automation scenarios, the calculator helped ground those discussions in something every organization cares about: business impact. It gave attendees a way to estimate how document automation could affect their own operations and helped move the conversation from technical possibilities to practical outcomes.

I think we were really taken aback by the diversity of perspectives in the room. People came from different industries and business environments, and brought very different operational realities and use cases to the discussion.

We were surprised that many of the issues being discussed were incredibly similar. Within all industries, companies had a lot of the same challenges around managing documents, approvals, integrations and keeping processes consistent. 

While working on practical document-processing scenarios, it became evident that the challenges most companies face go far beyond document extraction. As operations expand across multiple departments and teams, many organizations are still faced with fragmented workflows, repetitive manual validations, inconsistent document formats and processes that are becoming harder to manage.

And the workshop also reinforced something we often see when working with SAP technologies: real conversations happen once people can interact directly with a solution. The hackathon became much more practical and grounded after participants started to set up schemas, test classifiers and experiment with workflows. Rather than considering AI as something far away or theoretical, attendees began to consider how these solutions could realistically be integrated into their own operational environments.

Another important conclusion we arrived at was the importance of integration for successful AI adoption. Precise extraction models are crucial, no one will deny that, but they are just one part of the process. Companies also require workflows capable of adapting to operational changes, handling variations between document types, and collaborating effectively over time with existing systems.

Some Reflections

One of the most gratifying aspects of the workshop was to look back and see how quickly participants started to relate the exercises to their own organizations. Throughout the day the conversation evolved easily from examples under discussion to real business processes, internal priorities and potential next steps. Watching that transition in real time was a reminder of how the value of these sessions often goes far beyond the tech itself.

The workshop was also a great opportunity for us to hear directly from organizations that are facing different operational challenges, and to get a better understanding of how they are approaching document automation and AI adoption within their SAP landscapes.

Some of the most valuable insights came not only from the practical tasks but from the conversations that took place around them. The people who came had different perspectives, experiences and business contexts and we learnt from each other. The workshop was a valuable experience for all involved. As for me, I’m already looking forward to the next one, whether that’s as a host, a participant, or simply someone eager to keep learning.

entry.title: What a Document AI Workshop Taught Me About Clean Core
entry.content:
There is something I enjoy almost as much as writing content about SAP, and that is reading what people have to say in popular discussion forums regarding topics that may be seen as rather controversial. I think that many times, the fact that some themes generate so much discussion has more to do with people not really understanding them than with the proper complexity of the product, strategy or idea.

In the last couple of weeks, I read a lot of discussion around Clean Core, both in SAP Community and in these online forums. I was amazed by how people defined the concept. I mean, you could tell that the opinion they were communicating was, of course, directly linked to the way they each define Clean Core, and that is exactly what surprised me: everyone defined it differently. Some described Clean Core as a way to reduce customization, others associated it with cloud ERP, and a few questioned whether it was even realistic in large SAP environments. 

This reading coincided with the preparation and hosting of our hands-on SAP Document AI workshop together with SAP, although at first I didn’t see any connection between the discussions about Clean Core and the hackathon, by the end of the workshop, I found myself thinking about Clean Core in a way I hadn't before. 

In this case, our workshop was focused on a particular scenario involving document processing. Prior to the event, we had already created a Proof of Concept for one of our customers, who was working with a huge number of documents related to their operations from various regions and business units. The problem lay in the fact that it was not just about data extraction from invoices and tickets. Rather, the issue here was the high percentage of manual labor required for proper document classification, validation, and linking every file to the right business process. During our preparation for the presentation, we paid most of our attention to AI possibilities provided by SAP Document AI.

The unexpected aspect of the workshop discussions turned out to be the shift of focus to an area completely unrelated to what was being discussed in the first place. Naturally, people did show interest in the accuracy of document extraction, and in capabilities of AI as well. However, most of the questions were related to integration and scalability, how the solution could work in participants' environments, how well it would integrate with other applications, and how it would help them expand their capabilities without disrupting existing processes. 

Looking back, this is probably where the connection with Clean Core started to become clearer for me. Throughout the workshop, nobody was asking how to modify SAP so that Document AI could work. Nobody was discussing new custom tables, custom transactions, or large changes to core ERP processes. Several participants were already thinking about additional use cases, while others wanted to understand how the approach could scale across different document types and business units. The most prominent questions were adoption, integration, operational impact, and long-term scalability. The conversations focused on how to introduce a new capability while keeping the existing landscape stable.

That realization made me think about many of the online discussions I had been reading. A large number of them seemed to describe Clean Core primarily in terms of restrictions. The conversation often revolved around what organizations should avoid doing, what should not be customized, or what should be moved outside the ERP. While those aspects are certainly part of the concept, the workshop made me wonder if that perspective is too narrow.

What I saw during the event was not a limitation on innovation. If anything, it was the opposite. SAP Document AI was introducing completely new capabilities into existing business processes. The innovation was real, the business value was clear, and yet none of it required redesigning the ERP core itself. For the first time, I started thinking about Clean Core not as a strategy for reducing customization, but as a strategy for deciding where innovation should happen.

The more I thought about it after the workshop, the more I understood why so many people define Clean Core differently. Some people approach it from an architecture perspective, others from governance, and others from the realities of cloud ERP. Not one of these definitions can be wrong per se. But I found myself asking whether it is because of the lack of time spent on analyzing what the tool actually does for us and allows us to do.

In retrospect, what really got through to me at the workshop was not the demo as such. We had worked on getting ready the Proof of Concept, exercises and scenarios that would be demonstrated at the workshop during weeks in advance. What stayed with me after the workshop were the discussions triggered by all these. The most engaging ones did not concern the changes in ERP but rather organization of the processes that allowed introducing new functionality and retaining flexibility in the process. As much as I am not sure yet that there is such a definition of Clean Core accepted by all, after reading multiple threads online I am only even surer about this.

For me, Clean Core is no longer just about keeping systems standard. It is about creating the conditions for innovation to happen without making future change more difficult than it needs to be. Perhaps that is why I now see Clean Core less as a technical principle and more as a question every organization eventually has to answer: where should new capabilities live?

entry.title: SAP Document AI Workspace: Features You Need to Know
entry.content:
This blog is a comprehensive guide to navigating the Workspace UI — from document uploads and schema configuration to channels, workflows, and transport management.

The SAP Document AI Workspace is the new, full-featured environment for all document processing tasks on SAP BTP. From now on, all the latest SAP Document AI features are available exclusively in the Workspace UI — making it the recommended starting point for every new project involving document extraction, validation, and automation.

What Can You Do in the Workspace?

The SAP Document AI Workspace handles a wide range of activities throughout the document extraction lifecycle. It provides a centralized interface that reduces operational complexity, enhances process transparency, and accelerates deployment of document AI solutions across enterprise environments.

 

👤User Menu

The User Menu in the top-right corner of the Workspace lets you personalize your experience. From here you can change the display language (34 languages are supported), adjust UI theme options, and switch between client configurations.

💡 Tip: The Workspace supports localization in over 34 languages, making it accessible for global enterprise rollouts without additional configuration.

 

📂All Documents

The All Documents screen is your central hub for managing every document uploaded to the Workspace. It supports viewing, filtering, sorting, editing, and exporting, giving you full visibility across the extraction lifecycle.

Documents move through statuses: PENDING → PROCESSING → DONE (or flagged for manual review). Once confirmed, extracted data can be exported or pushed downstream to connected SAP systems.

🧩 Schemas

Schemas are at the heart of SAP Document AI. They define which fields to extract from a given document type — both header-level fields and line-item fields. The Workspace lets you view, enable, create, configure, and activate schemas.

SAP provides pre-trained schemas for common business documents including invoices, purchase orders, remittance advices, and more. For anything beyond these, you can build custom schemas leveraging Generative AI and LLMs — no template maintenance required.

✨ Instant Learning: As users confirm or correct extractions in the Workspace, the model learns immediately via few-shot prompting — improving accuracy on the next run without a separate training cycle.

 

📡Channels

The Channels feature manages how documents enter and leave the Workspace automatically. Inbound channels can monitor email inboxes (including Microsoft Exchange/Outlook via Azure app registration), while outbound channels handle notifications and API callbacks to downstream systems.

  • Inbound Email Channel: Register an Azure app with Mail.ReadWrite permissions, then map a specific email folder to a target schema in the Workspace.
  • Automatic Ingestion: When a document arrives in the monitored inbox, the Workspace extracts it according to the configured schema — no manual upload needed.
  • Outbound Callbacks: Configure webhook endpoints to push confirmed extraction results directly to SAP S/4HANA, SAP Build Process Automation, or any external system.

⚠️ Note: Email channel configuration requires an Azure AD application registration with appropriate mail permissions and a trust setup in your SAP BTP subaccount.

⚙️Workflows

Workflows let you automate complex, multi-step document processing tasks by chaining together the core Workspace capabilities. They can be triggered automatically via inbound channels — such as incoming emails — or invoked programmatically via the OData V4 API.

For example, a workflow can automatically extract data from an uploaded income statement, pass it to SAP Build Process Automation for business-rule evaluation, and create a Sales Order in SAP S/4HANA Cloud — all without manual intervention.


 


🚀Transports

The Transports feature integrates the Workspace with SAP Cloud Transport Management, enabling you to move schemas, workflows, and channel configurations across environments (development → test → production) in a governed, traceable way.

Schemas and model versions are first-class transport artifacts: they are versioned, can be activated or rolled back independently, and are fully transportable — reducing risk when promoting changes to production.

 

🛠Settings

The Settings menu contains configuration options that apply globally across all Workspace features. Key settings include:

⭐Best Practices & Key Takeaways

  • Use the Workspace UI for all new projects. The classic Basic UI will not receive new features going forward — all innovation is Workspace-only.
  • Subscribe using the -ias application plan when setting up entitlements in SAP BTP, as this provisions the Workspace interface.
  • Document quality matters. Invest in good scanning practices — OCR accuracy and extraction quality are directly tied to input file quality.
  • Design for human-in-the-loop. Even with high confidence scores, include a review step for business-critical data.
  • Leverage Instant Learning. Confirm or correct documents regularly to continuously improve extraction accuracy without retraining.
  • Use Transports for lifecycle management. Never configure production schemas manually — use the transport pipeline.

entry.title: Can AI Tell Your FI Ticket from Your MM Ticket? Does BYOM (Bring Your Own Model) helps?
entry.content:
The Problem: Which SAP Team Should This Ticket Go To?

If you've spent any time in an SAP support team, you would have lived this moment: a ticket lands with a subject like "PO stuck, please help urgently" via email or SNow and no indication of whether it's a Materials Management problem, a Finance problem, or a Integration Suite (CPI) interface quietly failing in the background. Someone, like us (SAP Full-Stack Developer) usually a lead/senior con has to read it, guess the module, and reroute it. Multiply that by a few hundred tickets a month and you've got a real cost hiding in plain sight. 

This is not a intro story, it is my real time experience I've had in work place.

Image generated by AI: Prompt: Can you create image for The Problem: Which SAP Team Should This Ticket Go To?

That is the question behind sap-ticket-classifier-bert: can a BYOM self sufficient, purpose-built AI model handle that first triage step instead of a person? Try it right now:

  • Live demo: https://karthionlupenix-sap-ticket-classifier-app.hf.space/
  • Model card: https://huggingface.co/KarthionLupenix/sap-ticket-classifier-bert

Why "Just Use ChatGPT" Isn't Enough

"Don't use a sledgehammer to crack a nut." It's tempting to point a large general-purpose LLM at every problem, but ticket routing is a narrow, repetitive, high-volume classification task, exactly the case where a small fine-tuned model beats a giant one on cost, latency, and predictability. You don't need a model that can write poetry to know that "GR/IR mismatch on vendor invoice" is an FI ticket. You need something fast, cheap to run, and consistent.

Here I propose BYOM (Bring Your Own Model) which can avoid huge token cost, and client specific data driven solution. 

const message = `AI Ethics & Responsibility: Think once again before using ChatGPT.
Estimates suggest that few conversations with ChatGPT may be associated with about 
500 mL (half a litre) of drinking water used for data center cooling.`;

console.log(message);

So the approach here is: take bert-base-uncased known open source base model,  add a classification head of neural networks, and fine-tune it specifically on SAP support ticket language.

Refer the technical details in the model card. 

Figure 1: SAP ticket classification flow using a fine-tuned BERT model

Nine buckets, one blind spot solved

The model sorts tickets into nine categories: FI, MM, SD, HR, ABAP, BASIS, INTEGRATION, OTHERS, and UNKNOWN.

That INTEGRATION category is the one most home-grown ticket systems get wrong. Middleware issues,

  • A CPI iFlow failing, an IDoc stuck in status 51,
  • An OData service timing out - don't cleanly belong to any one functional module, so they get bounced around before landing with the right team.

Giving integration its own first-class category means those tickets get to the CPI/PI team immediately instead of on the third handoff.

How well does it actually work?

On held-out test data, the model hits 82.4% overall accuracy and an 82.6% weighted F1 score. Some categories are close to solved, HR, INTEGRATION, and UNKNOWN tickets are classified correctly more than 94% of the time. The harder categories are MM and SD, sitting around 60–70% F1, which honestly matches reality: a lot of tickets genuinely straddle both (a delivery pricing issue touches MM and SD at once), so some confusion there isn't a bug, it's the nature of the taxonomy.

The part that matters more than the accuracy number

Any model trained on a small seed dataset will make mistakes once it meets real-world tickets. So the demo isn't a one-shot prediction tool — it has a built-in correction flow. When the model gets something wrong, you tell it the right answer on the spot. Those corrections get folded back into training with extra weight, so the model is continuously nudged toward the mistakes it's actually making, not hypothetical ones.

That loop : predict, correct, retrain. It is the difference between a cute demo and something you'd trust to sit in front of a real ticket queue.

Try it on your own tickets

Paste any ticket text into the live demo and watch it break down confidence across all nine modules. Or call it directly for inference. 

Try out - Colab

from transformers import pipeline

clf = pipeline(
    "text-classification",
    model="KarthionLupenix/sap-ticket-classifier-bert",
    revision="v0.2",
    top_k=None,
)

result = clf("Vendor invoice not posted due to GR/IR account mismatch")
# [{'label': 'FI', 'score': 0.93}, {'label': 'MM', 'score': 0.03}, ...]

top = max(result[0], key=lambda x: x['score'])
print(top["label"], top["score"])   # FI  0.93

Where this goes next

The current version is trained on 369 examples,  enough to prove the concept, not yet enough for blind production trust. The next step is fine-tuning it on real, anonymized ticket history from live SAP CIG/CPI integration work, which should push accuracy up meaningfully on the trickier MM/SD boundary cases.

If your team is sitting on years of SAP ticket history and still routing tickets by hand, this is a small proof that you don't need a massive model to fix that, you need the right labels, a focused model, and a feedback loop that keeps it honest.

The solution to this problem is "SAP Support Ticket Classifier — Arinous-0.2"  it identifies the correct team and owner for a ticket automatically. Try it.

Figure 2: Bring Your Own Model (BYOM) Arinous-0.2 deployed in HuggingFace

Author: If you find this information helpful, please consider clicking the "Like" button on this blog and sharing your thoughts in the comments section below. You can also connect with the author on their LinkedIn profile: [Author's LinkedIn Profile]'

Reference:

  1. https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/bring-your-own-hugging-face-transformers-in-sap-ai-foundation/ba-p/14264748 - [Credit to  @Marla-Ellinger]
  2. Is LLM fine-tuning really dead? - SAP Community  - [Credit to @Marla-Ellinger]
  3. https://dictionary.cambridge.org/dictionary/english/use-a-sledgehammer-to-crack-a-nut -[Credit to Cambridge]
  4. http://excalidraw.com/ -[Credit to exclidraw]
  5. https://huggingface.co/KarthionLupenix -[Credit to huggingface]
  6. https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1a5ssCv2vWgG_VkgLVeZNXm5nqYuJXUkU#scrollTo=HpR_RkUYDwTK-[Credit to Google CoLab]
  7. google-bert/bert-base-uncased · Hugging Face -[Credit to Google]
  8. Image Creation: Local diffusion model - Prompt: Can you create image for The Problem: Which SAP Team Should This Ticket Go To?

entry.title: Solving the Last Mile of Invoice-to-PO Line Matching in SAP MM & FI. Does traditional ML help here?
entry.content:
The Problem: A Month-End Nightmare

While working with an accounts payable team, I watched month-end turn into a nightmare in a PO-based MIRO world. Every open supplier invoice has to post before the books close, and leadership watches blocked and unmatched invoices like a Canis-Lupus.

So as the SAP specialist on the ground, I dug in. Turns out a big chunk of those stuck invoices had nothing to do with price or qty variance. They simply had no safe way to match invoice lines to PO line items  the invoice didn't mirror the PO's structure, the vendor never referenced PO line numbers, and SAP had nothing to propose a clean match from.

So it fell to a person. Invoice open on one screen, MIRO on the other, manually mapping every line to the right PO or GR before the system would even let it through. Multiply that by a stack of invoices at month-end, and a "standard" process turns into a grind nobody signed up for.

ChatGPT-Prompt: Can you please generate image for The Problem: A Month-End Nightmare in SAP MIRO, PO Invoice 2 PO Matching.

The Invoice Already Knows the PO. Just Not the Line.

Here's the thing nobody outside AP really understands about this problem: the invoice does tell you which PO it belongs to. UBL 2.1 and Peppol BIS 3.0 e-invoices carry the PO reference right there in the header 

<cac:OrderReference>PO-4500067890</cac:OrderReference> 
(OR)
<cbc:BuyerReference>PO-4500067890</cbc:BuyerReference>

So SAP knows the invoice is against PO-4500012345, PO-4500067890, and PO-4500099001.

What it doesn't know is which of the dozens of lines across those three POs each invoice line actually maps to. That mapping only exists at the header level. Line-level PO references are technically possible in the standard  I've seen maybe one vendor in the wild actually populate them. Everyone else leaves you to figure it out.

And "figure it out" usually means a human, squinting at two descriptions:

Invoice says

PO says

Hex Bolts M10

Hex Head Bolt M10x50mm Grade 8.8

Office Paper A4 500 sheets

A4 Copy Paper Ream 500

Same item. Zero words in common that matter. A junior AP clerk gets this right in about two seconds. A rules engine built on exact-match or Levenshtein distance gets it wrong every time, because the problem was never spelling  it's meaning.

  • Live demo: https://karthionlupenix-invmatchmaker.hf.space

Why Not Just Call an LLM?

The obvious answer today is "just call an LLM." It would probably match "Hex Bolts M10" to "Hex Head Bolt M10x50mm Grade 8.8" just fine if you asked it nicely. But an LLM call per invoice line, multiplied by every candidate PO line, across every invoice a company processes in a month, is slow and expensive for what is fundamentally a narrow, repetitive, high-volume matching task  not a reasoning task. You don't need something that can write you a poem to know two part descriptions refer to the same bolt.

Traditional machine learning doesn't get the hype right now. But for "does this invoice line mean the same thing as this PO line," it's the right tool, not the consolation prize.

The Practical Approach

The knowledge I picked up from University of Auckland's MAI course and Stanford's Machine Learning course pointed me toward a Multi-Layer Perceptron approach. This isn't a full AP automation suite there are enough of those already, and most are still doing header-level three-way match dressed up as "AI." I built specifically the part that's missing: given an invoice and the POs it already references, which PO line does each invoice line belong to.

Technical deep dive (skip ahead to "Why This Instead of Buying Off the Shelf" if you're not into the Machine Learning details)

Sentence-BERT (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, pre-trained, no fine-tuning yet) encodes both descriptions into 384-dimension vectors and takes the cosine similarity. That alone gets you most of the way "Hex Bolts M10" and "Hex Head Bolt M10x50mm Grade 8.8" land close together in embedding space even though they share almost no substring.

But semantic similarity alone isn't enough to post against, so I layer eight features on top of it per invoice/PO-line pair:

Feature

Description

Weight

F0

semantic_similarity

0.30

F1

quantity_ratio

0.15

F2

unit_price_ratio

0.12

F3

uom_match

0.08

F4

net_value_ratio

0.10

F5

material_number_match

0.10

F6

material_group_match

0.07

F7

vendor_material_match

0.08

Weighted sum, blended 40/60 with the raw semantic score, clipped to [0,1], sigmoid. Top-k (k=3) candidates per invoice line, greedy conflict resolution so two invoice lines don't both claim the same PO line, then triage:

  • ≥ 90% → auto-suggested, safe to post without a human touching it
  • 60–89% → goes to a reviewer, with the top-3 candidates shown so they're picking, not typing
  • < 60% → manual resolution  the AI is telling you honestly that it doesn't know

What's Still a Prototype (Being Upfront)

That weighted scorer is a stand-in. I'm calling it "LightGBM" in the docs because that's what it becomes in the next phase, but right now it's a fixed weight vector I hand-tuned against a handful of sample invoices not a model trained on labelled data. If you've read anything I've written before, you know I don't like shipping something and letting the framing overstate what it actually does. This is proof that the shape of the solution works, not a claim that the numbers above are production-grade.

The honest next step is the same one I took with the SAP ticket classifier I wrote about a few weeks back: get real labelled pairs actual accept/override/reject decisions from an AP team, or anonymized MIRO history and train an actual ranker on them instead of a vector I picked by eye. Everything else (the SAP MM field alignment, the INVOIC02 IDoc output, the triage thresholds) is close to done. The learning is the part that's still owed.

Why This Instead of Buying Something Off the Shelf

Most AP automation platforms are excellent at OCR and header-level tolerance matching, and I have no interest in rebuilding that. What they're mostly not built for is this specific line-level semantic step Rossum's own PO-matching extension, for instance, checks whether line items match by ID and field overlap, not by meaning. That's the gap this sits in: not a capture tool, not a three-way match engine, just the piece that answers "which line" once the header-level "which PO" is already known.

Related Work
This isn't the first attempt at line-level semantic matching. A 2020 paper, Online Similarity Learning with Feedback for Invoice Line Item Matching (arXiv:2001.00288), tackled the same gap using NER and taxonomy-based Jaccard similarity with a human-feedback loop, rather than embeddings. It's worth a read if you want to see how this problem was approached before Sentence-BERT-style models made semantic similarity cheap and fast.

Try It

If your team is still doing this matching by hand and you're curious what a semantic-first approach looks like on real SAP MM fields, the MVP is live invoice XML in, SAP-ready IDoc XML out, nothing hidden behind a demo request form.

Live demo and docs linked below. This is a Phase 1 proof of concept feedback on where it breaks is genuinely more useful to me than praise.

Author: If you find this information helpful, please consider clicking the "Like" button on this blog and sharing your thoughts in the comments section below. You can also connect with the author on their LinkedIn profile: [Author's LinkedIn Profile]'

Reference and Credits:

  • Machine Learning Course - @ Stanford, by Professor Andrew Ng
  • AI Fundamentals Course (COMPSCI 713) - @ University of Auckland, by Professors Thomas and Xinyu
  • ML Perceptrons: https://www.cs.auckland.ac.nz/~pat/706_98/ln/node134.html#SECTION00077000000000000000
  • Research Paper (Harnessing label semantics): https://arxiv.org/pdf/2212.01685
  • Levenshtein Distance: https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.5555/1822502
  • Cosine Similarity: https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/cosine-similarity
  • Cosine Similarity Image: https://machinelearningplus.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/10/soft-cosine.png
  • Sigmoid Activation: https://medium.com/data-science/sigmoid-activation-and-binary-crossentropy-a-less-than-perfect-match...
  • HuggingFace Follow on: https://huggingface.co/KarthionLupenix
  • Ollama Sentence-BERT: https://ollama.com/library/all-minilm:l6-v2
  • Peppol documentation: https://docs.peppol.eu/poacc/upgrade-3/profiles/63-invoiceresponse/
  • Image Generation: ChatGPT prompt - "Can you please generate image for The Problem: A Month-End Nightmare in SAP MIRO, PO Invoice 2 PO Matching."
  • Demo video link: https://youtu.be/XVqf4347mOI
  • Thanks: Former colleagues in Singapore, NZ, Germany, and India who shared their firsthand experience with the AP matching problem that started this. Thank you boys and girls. 🙂

entry.title: SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 3 in review
entry.content:
In my Day 2 recap, I showed what the Autonomous Enterprise looks like in Finance, CX, and Supply Chain.  All of it runs on one foundation: the SAP Business AI Platform. This is where agents get built, where they get context, and where they get governed. Day 3 was for the builders: the developers, architects, and technical consultants who turn this vision into reality.

Here's my recap of the day's sessions and the questions that came up most.

The morning briefing (recording) started with a provocative question: Is AI the new plastic? AI is the most overrated and underrated technology at the same time. Some people don't use it where they should. Others use it for everything without understanding its limitations or how to design these systems well. Plastic works the same way. It's an incredible material in medicine, and completely absurd when used to wrap a banana that already has a peel.

The message for Day 3: build AI intentionally. Know where it adds real value and if you're building agents, build the evaluation framework alongside them.

This is what we then saw in the new, fully managed Joule Studio, planned for general availability in Q3 2026. It introduces intent-based development: describe what you want to accomplish in natural language and Joule Studio guides the journey from business intent to production-ready agent. For example: We process 50,000 invoices each month with a team of 10. Most delays are due to matching issues and invoices being routed to the wrong approver. I'm looking for an automated solution. With one prompt, the agent handles classification, matching, routing, and exception escalation autonomously. Rewatch Step-by-step guidance to build custom Joule agents in Joule Studio to see how.

Answers to your most asked questions:

  • When is Joule Studio GA and how do I get access?
    GA is planned for end of Q3 2026. The Early Adopter Care program has started and registration is now closed. Trial access is being planned.
  • What happens to Joule Studio Classic?
    Joule Studio Classic continues with a free promotion as part of SAP Build. A migration path to the new, fully managed SAP Joule Studio is available that automatically converts your existing agents and skills into the new format which is the recommended path.
  • Do I need a technical background to use it? The intent-based development is designed for different levels of technical experience. Joule chat guides you through the entire solution or code generation with natural language.

The invoice demo in Joule Studio was no coincidence. Unstructured documents such as invoices, contracts, delivery notes, and quality certificates are where enormous amounts of business value get stuck. SAP Document AI (recording) is built to unlock them.

Did you know about the recent and upcoming feature releases in SAP Document AI?

  • Inbound and outbound channel support, which enables you to fetch the e-mail, extract both the e-mail body and attachments, such as Excel files. Follow the documentation to register an e-mail app here.
  • Standard extraction supports most document fields. But what about the ones specific to your business? With custom prompting you can write your extraction rules in plain language, choose your model, and combine with built-in extraction in the same schema. (Planned for Q3/2026)
  • With everyone talking about agents, how can agentic AI help with document processing? Agentic workflows shift document processing from a tool that reads to an agent that can reason and act when validating a payment advice note. (Planned for Q3/2026)

Answer to your most asked question:

  • What file types and document formats are supported?
    It depends on your license. The base edition covers the most common business document types. The premium edition extends to additional formats CSV, for example, is not included in the base edition. The full list is in the SAP Help documentation.

Custom prompting in SAP Document AI already hinted at it: you choose your model, you write your rules. That flexibility raises a bigger question: how do you access, evaluate, and govern all the models available to you across your entire AI landscape? That's what the session Build Your Own AI Solution with Generative AI hub answered. Rewatch it here.

Generative AI hub gives you access to leading frontier models like GPT-5.5, Claude 4.8 Opus, Gemini 3.5 Flash, Mistral Small 4, and SAP's own foundation models. The platform is built around one core insight: the best AI is not one model. Different tasks need different intelligence. The Evaluation Service is how you find the right fit and benchmark any model against your own data and metrics. Get started by finding the right model for the right task: first, find a use case that is visible and impactful. Then experiment, start small, iterate fast, and learn what works. Finally, operationalize, build the framework, define standards, and share what you've learned across your organization. Try it yourself with the free 30-day trial or continue with the on-demand CodeJam hands-on exercise “Getting started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core” on GitHub.

Answers to your most asked questions:

  • Where can I find the full list of supported models?
    See SAP Note 3437766 and the SAP Road Map Explorer for the latest model catalog, regional availability, and input/output token details. Use the Generative AI hub in SAP AI Core Calculator to estimate usage.
  • How is data security handled and where does my data go?
    SAP does not allow model providers to train on your data. SAP has contractual agreements with model providers (like OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, etc.) ensuring that customer prompts and completions are not used to train or fine-tune their models. All LLM calls are proxied through SAP AI Core / Generative AI hub.

One model on this platform deserves particular attention: SAP-RPT-1.5, the Relational Pre-trained Transformer. It is planned to become generally available with GenAI Hub 2607a, scheduled for July 20. The dedicated session explained why this matters: while LLMs are powerful for language-based tasks, they are not designed to work with tables. Real-world ERP, CRM, and SCM tables are typically not part of standard LLM training data. In addition, LLMs cannot reliably reason numerically across columns, which increases the risk of inaccurate or fabricated values when they are uncertain. This is unacceptable for financial and operational decisions. Rewatch SAP-RPT-1: The SAP Foundation Model for Structured Data to learn about the six adoption scenarios for SAP-RPT-1 that are planned to go live in the coming months. Most importantly, start testing with the free playground at rpt.cloud.sap.

Answer to your most asked questions:

  • When is RPT-1.5 generally available and what happens to RPT-1?
    RPT-1.5 is scheduled for GA in July 2026. RPT-1 deprecation is not yet scheduled and there will be sufficient migration time, and switching is straightforward as the APIs are almost identical.

The day closed with the very first virtual CodeJam "Build code-based AI Agents" where you learned in a training system how to build state-of-the-art AI agents using Generative AI hub, Python and LangGraph. You gained the skills to create custom tools for your agents, including leveraging SAP's RPT-1 model and deploying your agents to BTP. Rewatch Build code-based AI Agents and access all materials on GitHub to get hands on yourself anytime.

Day 1 set the foundation. Day 2 put it into practice. Day 3 gave you the tools to build it. Continue with the full series:
